data.title: BWX Technologies: Too Early For SMR (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

In January, I covered BWX Technologies (NYSE:BWXT), concluding that it was a lower-risk option in the SMR/nuclear segment, given its stable defense business and a still reasonable valuation at the time. Fast forward, and the SMR theme has reached hype levels, and BWX has seen its share price nearly double. I revisited the investment case and concluded that a downgrade to Hold is warranted.

Operating Model

As discussed in greater detail in my first article, BWX is primarily a defense contractor specializing in building and maintaining nuclear reactors for the US Navy (submarines) with highly stable and predictable contracts, revenue, and cash flow. On the commercial side, most of its revenue has come from uranium fuel fabrication, maintenance, and related services, with the Canadian Nuclear fleet (CANDU) its main customer. The launch and restart of traditional nuclear power plants in the US is also a source of added revenue, but what has the market and company excited is the construction and commercialization of SMRs (Small Modular Reactors), where BWX can apply its submarine reactor and uranium fuel know-how. It's part of the Pele project, which is testing the concept by building a 1.5MW nuclear power plant for the defense department. The company acquired Kinectrics for US$525m to increase its potential share in the SMR development. While I may fully agree that SMRs can be a substantial new growth avenue, the time to market is still years away and may be relevant in 2028 forward, while the fuel manufacturing could provide a new long-term revenue source, such as defense.

3Q25 Results

BWX should report a solid 3Q25 result with revenue trends accelerating on new contracts announced during the year, although the consensus expects a lower margin, possibly on revenue mix, more commercial vs defense, and cost inputs from recent raw material tariffs. The key data to look for is how the commercial business is evolving and how the recently closed Kinectrics acquisition is evolving.

Valuation

In the January valuation, I assigned a 2.1x PEG to BWX vs the consensus valuation of 2.4x PEG, which produced a US$153 price target for YE26 at 29x P/CE (cash earnings are net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation). The stock has surfed the SMR wave as well as added around US$300m to forward revenue on new contracts, mostly in the defense segment. Thus, the performance is partially fundamentally based.

The current consensus price target for YE26 is US$203, which continues to be valued at 2.4x PEG, and I have raised my fair value PEG to the same level, which indicates the shares are fully valued to YE26, barring new contracts or M&A.

It's not likely that the SMR buildout impacts the company's earnings in a meaningful way until 2028, and the margin gains may be a challenge.

Peer Comps

I gathered consensus growth and valuation metrics for BWX peers that include defense and energy stocks. As can be seen, BWX trades more like a defense stock, as it should with a rich or premium valuation. In the energy area it's expensive vs GE Vernova (GEV) or Siemens Energy (SMEGF, SMNEY), which I have covered recently and rate with buy. However, BWX is a far more palatable idea vs the pure SMR stocks such as Oklo (OKLO) and NuScale (SMR). In my recent Okolo analysis, it's evident that the market is working on optimistic if not exaggerated views divorced from reality. A good compromise could be Cameco (CCJ), which encompasses the full nuclear supply chain.

Risk

Not a lot can go wrong as a primary defense contractor to the US nuclear submarine fleet. Perhaps the primary risk may be technological obsolescence, where someone invents a fission reactor. Other risks may be related to raw material supplies and inputs that come under tariffs or embargoes. The main risk to the share price is if the SMR development does not proceed at a brisk pace, as is expected.

Conclusion

I downgraded BWXT to hold from buy. While the stock’s valuation has incorporated some of the SMR hype, medium-term earnings are grounded and should support the shares as we wait for SMR technology to prove itself and be commercially deployed.

data.title: BUG: The Structure, Characteristics, And Mechanics Of This Cybersecurity ETF
data.description:
The Global X Cybersecurity ETF (NASDAQ:BUG) has been around since October 2019. With a 100% technology holding and a nearly 60% allocation to the Top 10 stocks, this is a highly concentrated play on the cybersecurity market. Concentration is even more when you look at the small portfolio of just 24 stocks. The ETF benchmarks itself against the Indxx Cybersecurity Index, which has very specific inclusion criteria, so this helps us understand how the fund's portfolio is structured.

The nuts and bolts of the ETF are pretty straightforward: an AUM of over $1 billion, an affordable ER of 0.51%, the Global X name behind the fund, nearly $10 million in daily ETF share liquidity, relatively healthy interest in options, strong long-term momentum, market-matching performance, and a turnover well under control for such a volatile grouping of securities.

On the flip side is the high tracking error against the index (it's getting better over time, though), the lack of a dividend, a Hold rating from Seeking Alpha's Quant algo, and some signs of weakening momentum in the near-term.

Now that you've seen a snapshot, let's dive in further and learn more about how this fund is structured, what drives it, and how it has performed over the long term.

How is the Global X Cybersecurity ETF Portfolio Assembled?

It's important to know that the ETF's portfolio is constrained by what the index includes and excludes. It has a hard limit of 40 securities, which further exacerbates the concentration risk. In order to be eligible for inclusion in the index, the security must satisfy several conditions:

  1. It should have a market cap of no less than $200 million, and in cases where the free float is less than 10% of TSO (total shares outstanding), the free float portion should be worth at least $1 billion. Once a company is included in the index, it needs to maintain a market cap of only $80 million, and its average daily trading volume (ADTV dollar average) should stay above $1.2 million. The initial criterion for eligibility is a 6M ADTV of $2 million.
  2. Recently IPO'ed companies are eligible, but must have a market cap that exceeds half of the index's constituents - above the mean, in other words; in such cases, the IPO could be as recent as 10 days prior to what the fund calls its "selection date". For smaller market cap IPOs, the timeline is set to 30 days prior.
  3. The listing of the security can either be in an established market or an emerging one, which allows the ETF to hold a wider global selection of cybersecurity notables.
  4. If there are multiple share classes, the most liquid one is chosen for inclusion.

After the selection process for the index is done with, there's the question of weighting. This also follows a set of rules that govern individual weightings.

  1. The default range of weighting is 0.3% to 6%, but each security needs to maintain an ADTV of at least 60% of the initial eligibility criterion of $2 million, which is $1.2 million.
  2. In the event that there are several +5% weighting allocations to a set of securities, their total weighting cannot exceed 40%. In such cases, the cap for the remaining constituents drops to 4.5%, and any remaining weighting after all these allocations are completed are distributed between the non-capped securities in proportion to their individual market capitalizations.
  3. The fund is rebalanced and reconstituted every six months on days called Effective Days, which come around on the second Friday of May and November.

Now that we know how the fund's portfolio is guided by its benchmark index, let's look at the actual holdings.

This is more of a moment-in-time situation because the fund has a surprisingly high annual turnover of nearly 25%, but you're not likely to see securities drop in and out of the Top 10 on a frequent basis due to the stringent selection, inclusion, and retention criteria set out by the index.

When it comes to geographic allocation, we see a majority of the fund's $1.1 billion AUM concentrated in U.S. securities - about 72% or so. That's a significant portion that adds more on top of what I mentioned earlier, and it's something to note because some of the largest players in cybersecurity are domestic U.S. companies. The Top 10 holdings for BUG do have a couple of Israeli and Canadian companies (some of whom also have headquarters in the U.S.), but the composition of the Top 10 is a pretty good mirror of the whole portfolio of 24 stocks.

The Cyclicality of Cybersecurity and the Mechanics of BUG's Performance

The cybersecurity industry is notoriously news-sensitive, and if I whittle it down to its core, incident-sensitive. There's a ton of evidence that the industry moves up through what's known as "reactive spending", implying elevated outlays for cybersecurity when major breaches feature in a given news cycle or a major development necessitates the need to pivot from traditional service models.

The best example of this would be the post-pandemic surge that I captured below in the Nasdaq CTA Cybersecurity Index (NQCYBR) chart.

That's a doubling of the index right there, between the initial pandemic scramble for work-from-home cybersecurity implementations right up to the peak in November of the following year.

Similarly, you'll see several of these cyclical, almost peristaltic upward movements of the index as a whole, and this essentially reflects revenue uptrends during these phases of the cycle.

This example below with revenue growth percentages for BUG's biggest holdings is for that one period between March 2020 and November 2021.

The next major development that's already been with us for nearly three years now is the AI landgrab. That's not a nice way to put it, I know, but that's exactly what it is - an attempt by the deepest corporate pockets in the world to capture the bulk of AI infrastructure so every enterprise is forced to subscribe to an endless stream of AI-driven products.

The positive takeaway for BUG is that there's now an almost inexhaustible availability of capital from both institutional and retail investors who have pushed the valuations of these companies to closely mirror their hyper-scaler counterparts that are actually building out that infrastructure. I give you BUG's Top 3 again.

So, that's what moves the cybersecurity industry right now, and I don't see that changing for the next ten years, at the very least. That means risk will remain high for investors in this sector, but the reward is likely to be just as high, if not higher.

As long as the current upcycle lasts, we're likely to see these revenue and valuation trends moving in lockstep. The good news for BUG is that it's managed to keep pace with the market despite this cyclical behavior.

Some Additional Considerations for BUG

An investment in this fund involves a lot of risk, for sure, and some keen investors might even say it's not worth the higher amount of risk just to be able to match the performance of the market. That's up to each individual to decide, but the other side of that argument is that it can be used tactically to capture strong momentum on every upcycle of the cybersecurity market.

Another consideration might be the lack of dividend, which means you only have price action on your side and there's opportunity cost at play when you hold the ETF on the downcycles. The balancing view is that there aren't any immediate tax implications if you hold BUG long-term.

Finally, keep in mind that the volatility of this ETF and its individual holdings also opens up a world of options. Literally. That means you can take advantage of both upcycles and downcycles to make money, regardless of which way the industry is moving. Selling cash-secured puts gives you the optionality of owning more BUG or more of its components at attractive prices when the cycle is headed down; conversely, buying long calls works well during upcycles, allowing you to capitalize on momentum while still keeping your costs down.

Irrespective of what you decide to do, know that BUG is one way to tap into an essential, almost commoditized but highly sticky niche within the grander realm of AI-related technologies.

This article answers these three main questions about BUG:

  • Are there concentration concerns with the BUG portfolio?
  • Does BUG come with higher risks and higher potential rewards than the average ETF?
  • Is BUG, and the cybersecurity industry as a whole, susceptible to cyclicality?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: The Tragicomedy Of AT&T's Earnings
data.description:
Quarterly comedy

Each and every quarter the same comedy is staged in the telecommunications sector. While there is only one company that is really growing, all the five major competitors tout their great assets, their winning strategies and long-term growth prospects. They aim to reduce their massive debts, increase FCF and dividends, while gaining hundreds of thousands new subscribers.

Indeed, they gain subscribers. For example, AT&T (NYSE:T) touted 405,000 Postpaid Phone Net Adds in its Q3/25 earnings release, while incrementing its Fiber subscriber count by another 288,000 and its "Internet Air" (fixed wireless access or FWA) subscribers grew by a similar 270,000.

Over the long term, the quarterly growth bonanza adds up: From the end of 2020 until the most recent quarter, Postpaid Phones grew by 14% or roughly 10 million phones. Fiber subscribers more than doubled. Internet Air went from zero to 1.3 million subs.

This is the growth AT&T underscores in its earnings releases, this is what sell-side analysts apparently care about, and this is what drives short-term stock market reactions. Over the long term, what really matters is the following, which is somewhat hidden among the numbers:

  • Postpaid ARPU grew far below CPI over the past five years. CPI was roughly 25%, while ARPU grew only 2%. I.e. all that wonderful subscriber growth came at the expense of inflation-adjusted lower prices.
  • Yes, Fiber is growing, but legacy broadband is shrinking. Overall, broadband (DSL, Internet Air, Fiber) connections grew just 6% over 5 years. I.e. the massive fiber investment only compensated for the losses of legacy broadband connections. And without the 1.3 million FWA subs the total category would have shrunk. Yes, that's right, Fiber alone doesn't make up for DSL losses. The only broadband service that is really growing right now is FWA (which is why T-Mobile (TMUS) is the only major growth telco).
  • Thanks to much higher Fiber ARPU, total broadband revenues including DSL grew 14%, but AT&T has to share the economics with its fiber financing partners. The fiber part has grown from $800m to $2.2B, while the non-fiber broadband part has been shrinking from $1.4B to 0.9B from 2021 until 2025. Since we must assume that a large chunk of the fiber growth effectively belongs to the JV partners, it is highly likely that the broadband revenue "owned" by T shareholders has actually shrunk (or close to it) over the past five years – despite all those huge fiber investments.

As a result, despite the outright comical "growth festival" staged on quarterly earnings dates, AT&T's quarterly Communications Adjusted EBITDA has grown 15% over the past five years – again far below CPI.

Quarterly outlook

What made things worse for AT&T in this quarterly earnings release was a lackluster outlook. After several quarters of net debt reduction, financing for the recent $23B spectrum acquisition will significantly increment indebtedness again, which will likely limit dividend growth in the near- to mid-term. In addition, the planned extra contribution to the company's pension plans to the tune of $1.5B until the end of 2026 will further drain available cash.

Finally, during the Q3/25 earnings call management has effectively confirmed that it accepts lower ARPU in exchange for absolute revenue growth:

In order to grow service revenue, we have to be willing to also target other places where we're underpenetrated. And as John effectively laid out, that is a part of our strategy. But it doesn't mean that going down ARPU is at the sacrifice of overall service revenue. We are trying to maximize service revenue.

As a result, there is nothing in these results that would made us expect anything else than just more of the same: Price pressure coupled with growing subscriber numbers will lead to very modest earnings growth, if any.

Tragic conclusions

My conclusions for AT&T have always been similar: It simply is a bad business. Over the long term it barely manages to keep its technology up to date (albeit at a huge cost) and roughly grows with CPI at best, as general category growth compensates for inflation-adjusted lower ARPU. An important further conclusion is that in the case of non-growth stocks entry points matter more than anywhere else. Investors that buy at the wrong price will have a hard time recovering their investment.

In my opinion, now is such a time. AT&T has had its moment under the sun as it simplified its business, shed distractions like the movie and TV business, and became a lot more predictable. Considering that it is trading close to the top of the past five years' range, while results have been nothing but stable and trading multiples have grown above their multi-year averages across all relevant metrics, I just don't see how the stock can outperform from here with higher debt, lower ARPU and more of the fiber cake taken away by JV partners.

data.title: 10%+ Yielding Stocks Too Cheap To Ignore Right Now
data.description:
Typically, when a stock offers a 10% yield, it means that either the market does not believe the yield is sustainable or that the company is unlikely to deliver much in the way of growth. This is because the market, on average over the long term, tends to deliver between 8% and 12% annualized total returns. Therefore, when a dividend yield reaches 10% or higher, it means that it really does not need to deliver anything in the way of growth or valuation expansion to still produce an attractive total return. That being said, every now and then, there are yields at this level that present genuinely attractive risk-adjusted total return potential for investors, and in this article, I am going to discuss two of them. For those who would prefer a 6-8% yield with 10%+ annualized dividend growth instead of a 10%+ yield with a bit slower growth, check out my recent article here.

Delek Logistics Partners (DKL): A Decade Of Distribution Growth

The first one is DKL. It is an MLP (AMLP) that issues a K-1 and owns a diversified portfolio of midstream infrastructure, including a crude oil segment with 1,188 miles of crude and refined product pipelines, a water business that includes about 1.6 million barrels per day of water disposal capacity, and a natural gas business with over 200 million cubic feet per day of gas processing capacity. Its assets are primarily concentrated in Texas, particularly in the Permian Basin.

The company has grown its distribution remarkably consistently over time, with 50 consecutive quarterly distribution increases. Today, it offers a very attractive yield of 10.2% on a next-12-month basis. The company does not need to generate much in the way of distribution growth to still deliver an attractive annualized total return. However, distributable cash flow ("DCF") per unit growth is projected to be much more appealing, with a 4.6% CAGR over the same period.

Management expects to grow in a manner that increases its distribution coverage ratio to make it more sustainable over time while also working to deleverage.

On that note, DKL’s coverage ratio during the second quarter was 1.22x, and management expects it to continue rising through the remainder of the year as growth projects come online and drive additional cash flow to the bottom line. This, in turn, should enable DKL to bring down its leverage ratio and further strengthen the company’s balance sheet and distribution sustainability.

When you combine these factors with the fact that the company’s midstream assets are highly contracted and deliver stable cash flows even during suppressed energy (XLE) price environments, DKL looks like a very attractive combination of yield and growth that should also deliver capital appreciation as the market grows increasingly confident in the sustainability of its payout and the strength of its balance sheet.

At around 8.65x EBITDA with significant EBITDA growth ahead, DKL looks attractively priced. The biggest risk with DKL is that it is highly dependent on Delek US Holdings (DK), though management is actively addressing this by reducing interdependence between the two entities. As that progress continues, we expect DKL units to re-rate higher. In the meantime, investors receive an attractive, sustainable, and growing yield that can deliver strong returns while waiting for this to take place.

Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL): A Defensive 11.75% Yield

The other high-yielding stock I want to talk about today is MSDL. It currently pays a base dividend of $2 per share, which equates to an 11.75% dividend yield relative to the current stock price. MSDL is a business development company (BIZD), which means it has been under selling pressure recently along with the broader BDC sector, though it has actually outperformed peers in recent weeks. MSDL remains significantly undervalued, trading at about a 17% discount to its net asset value, despite having very similar quality metrics to peers like Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), which trades at a much narrower (3%) discount to NAV despite recently underperforming MSDL.

The reason I like MSDL is because, in addition to its very attractive dividend yield, it has a defensively positioned business model, with nearly all of its investments in first lien senior secured loans. It also has one of the lowest non-accrual rates in the entire BDC sector. Perhaps best of all, MSDL charges some of the lowest fees among externally managed BDCs, and it is managed by Morgan Stanley (MS), giving it considerable proprietary economic insights, underwriting capabilities, and deal flow. Additionally, it has among the lowest payment-in-kind ("PIK") rates among BDCs, meaning that its loans tend to be higher quality and it receives a greater percentage of its net investment income ("NII") via cash interest payments than many peers do.

Last but not least, its leverage ratio is in a solid position (1.10x on a net debt to equity basis) with significant capacity remaining before it reaches its 1.25x upper limit. Overall, its balance sheet is strong, with an investment grade credit rating and no near-term low-interest-rate debt maturing, thereby limiting headwinds from higher interest costs. In fact, since much of its debt is floating rate, as the Fed continues to cut rates—as is widely expected—it should benefit from lower interest expenses on the liability side of its balance sheet.

While this all sounds incredible—combining a near 12% dividend yield backed by an investment-grade balance sheet, a favorable debt maturity profile, a defensive investment portfolio, and potential for valuation multiple expansion given its discount to NAV—there are a few concerns to keep in mind. The first is that as the Fed cuts interest rates aggressively, MSDL’s NII will likely decline. While much of its debt is floating rate, so are the vast majority of MSDL’s investments. Therefore, when the Fed cuts rates, its interest income will also decline, and because it has more floating-rate exposure on the asset side than on the liability side, NII is expected to fall.

Additionally, there have been some negative headlines in the private credit space following a few high-profile bankruptcies. Fortunately, none appear directly related to MSDL, but they have raised broader questions about underwriting standards across the industry. Finally, this decline in NII could pressure MSDL’s ability to maintain its base dividend, given that it generated $0.50 per share of NII last quarter (exactly in line with its dividend payout).

However, there are reasons to believe that MSDL may be able to sustain its dividend for at least another quarter. Its earnings power was understated last quarter due to higher leverage during the period, and the full benefit of that leverage should flow through in subsequent quarters. Additionally, it still has further balance sheet capacity to add modest leverage to offset these headwinds. Management also refinanced some debt at lower interest rates during the quarter, which should further support NII per share going forward.

For all these reasons, I believe MSDL’s dividend is potentially sustainable for at least another quarter, depending on the pace of Fed rate cuts and management’s philosophy regarding leverage and dividend coverage. Even if MSDL ultimately elects to trim its base dividend, as long as its underwriting performance remains strong—which all indications suggest—it is likely to be a modest reduction, keeping the yield on cost near or above 10%.

Therefore, I think the total return profile for MSDL remains highly attractive, even assuming NII per share declines 10% to 20% in the coming quarters, as long as its NAV per share remains stable. Its dividend yield should stay at a 10%+ level relative to current cost, and over time, I expect the market to assign it a valuation more in line with BXSL's, which implies significant upside potential for MSDL shares.

Investor Takeaway

Between DKL and MSDL, investors can access double-digit yields that should continue to provide a yield on cost of at least 10% for the foreseeable future. Both are fairly defensively postured, meaning that cash flows and distributions should remain strong even in the event of an economic downturn. Additionally, both enjoy potential for valuation multiple expansion as their management teams continue to execute and market sentiment improves. Such powerful combinations of double-digit yields and significant capital appreciation potential are rare in the market today. As such, I am loading up on both at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Rocket Lab: Why The Neutron Could See More Demand Than You Think
data.description:
Thesis

Rocket Lab (NASDAQ:RKLB) shares have soared in recent times. It wasn't too long ago at the start of the year when you could have picked up shares in the low $16 range. Today, shares are pushing $70. As the company continues growing, the hard part for investors has been trying to price in just how much increased revenue we could see from the Neutron when it comes to market. I see Rocket Lab following a similar model to that of the Electron when it comes to scheduling Neutron launches/clients. That is, filling in the gap for those who have too much to launch on their own or need a very quick, reliable alternative for getting payloads into orbit. With the Electron, many of their clients are repeat customers trying to build out satellite constellations. However, this clientele is limited only to those with satellite payloads of less than 300kg. Now, with the Neutron, I see no shortage of customers adopting the new rocket for their own constellation build-out, as I'll explain. I've identified 15 potential clients for the Neutron already, which makes me bullish on its potential adoption within the medium space lift range. We should see more upside to this stock leading into earnings. The Neutron is becoming more of a competitor than we think, filling in the gap for those that don't need the whole capacity of larger rockets like the Falcon 9.

Japan is turning to Rocket Lab to unload its space pipeline

The recent news out of Rocket Lab is a fresh contract with Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA) and also a repeat customer, Synspective, for the small lift market. Synspective is a Japanese private space company that specializes in Synthetic Aperture Radar satellites, so anything from data services to analytics. They’re a pretty good repeat customer for Rocket Lab; satellites are often launched in constellations and require a few trips for completion. For investors, repeat launches with the same company are what we want to see, be it Black Sky (BKSY), iQPS, or Synspective.

The other newly signed contract is with JAXA for two dedicated Electron launches. It's a nice step in the direction of more international collaboration. In recent years, we’ve seen Japan's space industry continue to rely on Rocket Lab for access to orbit. Both missions are scheduled for late 2025 and into early 2026. They’re again set to lift off from Rocket Lab’s Launch Complex 1 in New Zealand. It’s nice to see Rocket Lab operate on multiple fronts, be it in New Zealand or Virginia. The first mission will deploy JAXA’s RAISE-4 satellite, which is tipped to include eight innovative technologies on the satellite itself, developed across Japan’s private sector. The launch contract also comes with some small tailwinds out of Japan; it is part of JAXA’s broader effort to accelerate its tech maturation via real in-orbit testing opportunities.

Now, JAXA, of course, can launch their own rockets; they hold the H-IIA, H-IIB, and the new H3, along with the smaller Epsilon launcher. But coming to Rocket Lab is more about cost efficiency and a quick response. You see, most of Japan’s rockets are pretty overused, with missions ranging from ALOS-4 or planetary probes to national defense interests. We even saw their Epsilon rocket program, designed for small payloads, face technical and scheduling challenges. Back in 2022, the Epsilon-6 launch failed, and the program underwent a full redesign, which led to some delays in its successor, Epsilon S, now targeting 2026. So it's great to see Rocket Lab moving in here to pick up the slack. What we want as investors moving forward is for Rocket Lab to be the next in line, the least effort option that can get a payload into the air as quickly as possible. Launching with Rocket Lab would also come with a much better cost for them, with the Electron carrying a price tag of about $7 to $10 million, meaning the RAISE-4 doesn't even need to wait in line or share ride space that compromises its orbit or schedule. JAXA has also signaled that more launches are to come; they appear to have a number of small payloads that need launching, so they have sought multiple launches from Rocket Lab. The second Electron mission is set to carry a diverse rideshare payload of eight spacecraft. This includes educational smallsats, an ocean-monitoring satellite, a compact multispectral imaging demonstration, and even an origami-folded deployable antenna designed to expand to 25 times its stowed size.

Private sector demand

At the same time, Rocket Lab’s launch momentum this year is still going strong after the successful “Owl New World” mission for Synspective this month. It was the 73rd Electron launch and the 15th of the year. The mission carried Synspective’s seventh StriX Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) satellite to about 583 km in orbit, adding to the company’s constellation. This launch was actually the first of 21 new dedicated Electron missions planned through to the end of the decade, which is set to bring Synspective’s total launches with Rocket Lab to 27. For many small private companies, we’re seeing the Electron as the backbone of their constellation deployment strategy. We’ve heard from CEO Peter Beck in the past. He wants a competitive launch market and for everyone to turn to the most reliable launcher, in his case, Rocket Lab. Now, whether these launches are private or governmental, he doesn’t care; he just wants them to see the Electron and eventually the Neutron as the best option in terms of cost, time to orbit, and reliability.

So, what investors should be thinking about is whether this kind of private sector/government demand can transfer over into the Neutron when it finally comes to market. The short answer for me is yes, there are actually dozens of commercial companies and even government agencies that currently need/want medium-lift launch capacity. So we’re talking about roughly 1 to 13 tons to LEO, or even an affordable direct injection to interplanetary trajectories, which is a tough ask for other launch providers. When Rocket Lab’s Neutron becomes commercially available, it should be set to fill in that gap. It's designed for about 13 tons to LEO and 1.5 tons to Mars/Venus. I’m expecting a clear shift here: more medium-class satellites on dedicated, responsive flights and more commercial interplanetary missions. With governments diversifying away from a handful of large providers.

A long list of potential customers

Here’s where I see the early demand coming from. I’m going to prioritize operators and primes with already published satellite masses or an explicit constellation/mission program that either exceeds small-launcher classes or would actually benefit from direct interplanetary injection. So this includes broadband GEO/MEO operators, large LEO broadband programs, major EO primes, on-orbit servicing firms, and national science agencies that are currently running small interplanetary work.

The first area I see interest in is Telesat’s Lightspeed constellation or similar programs. It’s one of the most ambitious medium-Earth-orbit (MEO) broadband networks in development right now. They want global, low-latency connectivity using around 198 satellites to achieve this, and here's the key part: each weighs roughly 700 to 750 kilograms. These satellites are far too heavy for small-lift rockets like Electron but fit very nicely within the Neutron’s payload class, especially for clustered deployments of a few satellites per launch. Now, Lightspeed’s initial deployment phase actually began with contracts to SpaceX’s Falcon 9. But it’s going to require dozens of launches throughout the next decade to build and refresh the network. The company will obviously want a competitive launch market here, and once the Neutron becomes operational, it should make it an ideal option for follow-on deployment waves or regional replenishments in my view.

Next up, I see Eutelsat OneWeb’s satellites, which weigh in at roughly 150 kg each, as being a key customer moving forward. Now, while 150kg seems small, keep in mind the company's needs hundreds of satellites and refreshes to aid their constellation. A neutron would see multiple satellites launched. So many missions require medium-scale manifests, where multiple satellites are deployed in a single dedicated flight, or even mixed-mass campaigns where small and slightly larger payloads are combined. There are also a few very well-known players I see being in the mix, starting with Amazon (AMZN). They have Project Kuiper satellites, each about 570 kg, that sit well above the smallsat class and require way more substantial lift capacity. Right now, Kuiper has secured rides on other vehicles like Ariane 6, but their need for dedicated medium-lift launches is growing rapidly. Particularly for batches of satellites that must reach precise orbital slots for a phased constellation deployment.

Elsewhere, we have SES’s O3b mPOWER satellites. They each weigh a hefty 1,000 to 1,200 kg. They’re part of a next-generation MEO constellation for high-throughput/low-latency connectivity across some underserved regions. That kind of size places them well in the medium-class satellite category. Using Neutron for deployment would get SES to launch multiple satellites per mission, give them the precise orbital placement, which is a must, and speed up the constellation launch.

You see, the Falcon 9 payload is massive at over 22 tons, and many clients actually don’t need that much capacity, so we often see the rocket loaded up with a lighter capacity. So a Neutron medium alternative should reduce dependence on larger, more expensive heavy-lift rockets. Staying on satellites, Viasat’s ViaSat-3 satellites are some of the largest commercial communications satellites. They carry launch masses exceeding 6,000 kg right now. Viasat also has an ongoing roadmap, which includes prototype satellites, regional backup platforms, and experimental payloads. They should be in search of medium-class launch options in the near term.

What I'm trying to say here is that there will be no shortage of demand for the Neutron once it comes to market. We should expect to see a very similar commercialization to the Neutron. A focus on clients who require full constellations or long-term launch relationships. I’ve put together a table below of 15 key players that I believe could all turn to Rocket Lab for their payload launches in the future.

Valuation

Rocket Lab is trading in the $68 range with a market capitalization of roughly $32.07 billion. If we take a look at valuation metrics, the company holds a forward EV to sales ratio of 53.89x. It’s dramatically higher than the sector median of 2.15x, so Rocket Lab shares are at an extreme premium relative to typical sector peers, which is no surprise. Similarly, the forward price to sales ratio of 54.32x is way above the sector median of 1.69x. There are obviously expectations here for significant growth. The market is trying to put a price on the Neutron and what kind of demand it should see when it comes to the market. There is also likely to be a bit more growth priced in than the Neutron launches alone. Rocket Lab’s main business actually comes from its space systems, which are also expected to grow. Remember, Rocket Lab here is an end-to-end space company, so they can build what you want to get launched and launch it too. And with multiples such as these, its current valuation is highly stretched compared to other players, and future performance needs to justify this substantial premium. But here’s why I think it will.

I’m going to model a scenario, similar to what we’ve seen in the past with the Electron, where a company requires multiple launches to complete their constellation. Estimates for the price of the Neutron can be a mixed bag, but most seem to agree on a $50 million price tag with room to go up. I'm going to stick to that range of $50 to $55 million in my scenario. If we take the Telesat Lightspeed Constellation, they have about 298 satellites planned in the long run with satellite masses in the 700 to 750 kg range, so a 0.725 ton average. They need them in MEO or about 8,000 km up in space. Let's also assume that with Rocket Lab’s Neutron, they can pack 13,000 kg to LEO with MEO insertion, reducing payload slightly, so assume 10 tons per launch for a realistic Telesat insertion. If we then calculate the number of Neutron launches over the next decade, the number of satellites per launch comes in at roughly 10 tons ÷ 0.725 tons, so 13 to 14 satellites per launch. With a total of satellites in the pipeline at 298, we could see the number of launches required come to 22. Not a figure that seems out of this world, considering we see these numbers when it comes to Electron launches. It puts the full price tag over the long run in the $1.1 to $1.21 billion range. So it's clear investors are right to be unsure of how to price Rocket Lab shares, which is why we’re seeing the price of the stock slowly climb. Any good bit of news, be it the launch site coming to completion in Virginia or Peter Beck mentioning the Archimedes engine is being hot-fired every day, adds a nice bit of momentum to the stock.

The real risk is market penetration

Now, I’ve laid out quite the compelling opportunity in the medium-lift market. But the real concern for me will be market penetration after the Neutron comes to fruition. The revenue and profitability assumptions rely on securing those high-value customers like Telesat, OneWeb, and others I mentioned. Profitability in the launch industry is all about reputation and long-term relations. Many of Rocket Lab’s record-setting launches this year have been repeat customers. It’s recurring revenue we expect to show up each earnings under that launch revenue figure. If we don't see early adoption from more established players, the projected launch pipeline could fall short. After all, it’s a completely new launch vehicle, so the Neutron will face scrutiny around reliability/performance just like the Electron did when it first launched. Even with their current reputation, customers may be a bit hesitant to commit high-value satellites to a rocket that has not yet demonstrated multiple successful flights. Adding to this, there's competition from already established medium- and heavy-lift providers like SpaceX, Arianespace, and Blue Origin.

All eyes on earnings next

Earnings are set to fall around November 10th, sometime after the bell. Analysts anticipate an earnings per share of roughly -$0.10, so still more of a focus on growth and expansion despite the losses. The biggest mover of the stock on that earnings call will be management's updates on the Neutron. We should hear about how the Archimedes Engine Testing is going and Flight Hardware Integration. On top of this, a proper launch window should be given with just a couple of months left in 2025.

data.title: OFS Credit: I Would Only Consider Short-Term Preferreds
data.description:
Introduction

While I generally stay away from CLO equity investments, there are senior securities issued by CLO equity CEFs that are potentially interesting. Not in the least because these securities tend to have a minimum coverage ratio. OFS Credit Company (NASDAQ:OCCI) is a CET that predominantly invests in CLO Equity, which represented almost 92% of the total portfolio at the end of July. CLO Equity is great when everything goes right, but it’s the lowest quality tier when investing in CLOs, and a deteriorating credit quality could mean an increase in defaults, and that doesn’t bode well for CLO Equity investments.

That’s the main reason why I will focus on a more senior security in OFS Credit’s capital stack.

The NAV is decreasing fast

In order to determine the risks and the appeal of the senior securities in OCCI’s capital structure, I first need to have a look at the recent quarterly results of OFS Credit Company.

As you can see below, OCCI recorded an interest income of $11.9M in the third quarter, on which it generated a net investment income of $6.05M. As the company’s only financial liabilities are term preferred shares, it has to record the preferred dividends as interest expenses.

This means the preferred dividend coverage ratio in the third quarter was approximately 381%. And looking at the 9M results, the coverage ratio was approximately 395%. It is clear OFS Credit is in a good position to cover its preferred dividends.

The common units are worse off. Despite a $16.4M net investment income, OFS recorded in excess of $17M in realized and unrealized losses on its portfolio in the first nine months of the year. This, in combination with the dividend payments and the continuous increase of the total amount of shares outstanding, has resulted in a pretty severe erosion of the NAV/share, which dropped from $7.24 in July 2024 to just $6.13 by the end of July of this year. And in a subsequent update, OFS Credit mentioned it now expects its NAV to be between $5.50 and $5.60 per share at the end of September. This means it doesn’t look promising for the October NAV, which is when OFS’s financial year ends.

OCCIO: a six-month investment

As you can see below, OFS Credit has five series of preferred stock outstanding, of which three series are publicly traded. I’m mainly interested in (NASDAQ:OCCIO) as this series of term preferred stock benefits from the ‘seniority by maturity’ principle (it goes without saying that a substantial deterioration in the credit markets will put all preferreds at risk, even the near-maturity issues). The mandatory repayment date is just six months from now, and as OCCIO is trading at a discount to its call price of $25, it paves the way to generate a combination of (preferred) dividend income as well as a small capital gain.

The Series C term preferred stock was issued in 2021 and offers a 6.125% preferred dividend coupon. The preferred dividends are payable on a monthly basis, and this works out to $0.1276 per preferred share per month. This specific security has been callable since April 2023, but OFS Credit obviously didn’t call it, as the returns on CLO Equity were very high in the past few years and a 6.125% cost of equity is pretty low. But as it is a term preferred share, there also is a mandatory call date, which has been set at April 30, 2026.

This means that anyone buying the security now will receive six additional monthly dividends of $0.1276 per month while also receiving the $25 cash payment in April. Given the current share price of $24.92, this represents a total return of 3.4% in almost exactly six months. Every cent makes a difference, so I wouldn’t necessarily buy at $24.92 considering the stock is up 11 cents right now. But putting in an order at, for instance, $24.85 would result in a total return of approximately 3.7% in six months.

An additional interesting feature is the mandatory asset coverage ratio. CEFs have to provide a 200% asset coverage ratio on their preferred equity. At the end of the first semester of the current financial year, OCCI’s coverage ratio was approximately 238%, and this further improved to around 250% by the end of the third quarter (which ended in July). The mandatory coverage ratio is interesting, but it isn’t a guarantee: when the situation on the credit markets deteriorates fast, asset values may fall, and it may even be difficult to get a [fair] quote for CLO equity instruments.

Investment thesis

While I’m not interested in owning the common stock of OFS Credit, I think the more senior securities could be worth the additional attention. OCCIO is an interesting example of a short-term senior security, as the mandatory 200% asset coverage ratio provides an interesting protection mechanism.

I currently have no position in any of OFS Credit’s common or preferred stock, but I will keep an eye on the preferred stock issued by OCCI as long as the coverage ratio doesn’t rapidly deteriorate.

data.title: Nvidia: The Big 3 Issues Before Earnings
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) is set to report earnings on the 19th of November, and this may be the most important earnings yet for Nvidia and the AI industry.

A lot has been happening over the last few months that has shaken up the market, and it will be very interesting to see what Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has to say on the matter.

Nvidia faces three major issues as we approach the next earnings release, and these will be key points of discussion in the coming earnings call.

All in all, I see significant risk factors as we head into earnings, with a lot of room for Nvidia to disappoint.

With that said, an earnings overreaction could be a good place to add some exposure.

China: From Hero to Literal 0

This is something investors have been wondering for quite some time now.

China used to account for over 20% of Nvidia’s data center revenue, and though there has been hope in the last quarter that this could be salvaged with the right trade deals and products, it is now pretty much official that China is no longer an Nvidia fan.

As much was said by Jensen Huang in a recent interview with Citadel Securities.

We went from 95% market share to 0%, and so I can’t imagine any policymaker thinking that that’s a good idea, that whatever policy we implemented caused America to lose one of the largest markets in the world,

Source: TechRadar.

The good news is that, in theory, the current guidance was already not taking into account any potential revenue from China.

Current estimates have Nvidia at $54.66 billion for Q3. This is still a healthy 17% QoQ growth.

Assuming around 80-85 % of total revenue comes from Data Centers, then we’d be looking at $45 billion for the segment.

This would equate to QoQ growth of 9% which, to be fair, would be a re-acceleration compared to Q2. However, we must also consider that Q3 has usually been a seasonally strong quarter for Nvidia.

Is Competition A Problem?

Okay, the China thing may be priced in. But what about the recent increase in competitive alternatives to Nvidia?

As I highlighted in my most recent Nvidia article, companies like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) are beginning to catch up in terms of performance.

The MI450 threatens to take market share from the Blackwell.

But of course, Nvidia is not going to sit idly by and let competitors take their market.

In this regard, we should expect to see a lot of talk about the next levels of the AI revolution.

Specifically, I’d like to update on NVLink Fusion, Nvidia’s recently launched custom silicone which enables Nvidia chips to be used with others in order to create custom AI data centers.

Beyond that, I/m sure there will be plenty of talk on the outlook for robotics and even quantum.

We, in fact, have the Nvidia GTC coming up in Washington D.C, which will give us a taste of what comes next.

Is AI A Bubble?

Lastly, I’m sure the AI bubble notion will be addressed at some point. Since Nvidia’s last earnings, there has been a mountain of deals which many have deemed to be circular and irresponsible.

Not only do I want to hear Jensen talk about the deals Nvidia has made, but also his outlook on companies like OpenAI (OPENAI) and the deals they have struck with companies like AMD and Oracle (ORCL).

As has been pointed out recently here on Seeking Alpha, Nvidia has a very robust and growing portfolio of AI ventures.

But this could become a double-edged sword if AI valuations peak.

Jensen should lay out a clear strategy of what the company is seeking to achieve with these investments.

Buying The Earnings Dip?

How does this all translate into earnings?

Here’s what I want to know:

  • Have Nvidia’s margins peaked given the increased competition?

  • Does the company have any hope of recovering China revenues?

  • Any new insights on Rubin’s successor, Feynman?

  • And with all the deals already in place to expand capacity, is there really room for more?

Hopefully Jensen will provide some clarity here.

Ultimately, though, I think there’s a pretty nice set-up for a selloff. Earnings so far have faced increased scrutiny from investors.

Netflix (NFLX) plummeted 10%, Tesla (TSLA) is down after earnings, and the broader stock market has been quite volatile in the last few weeks.

With all this uncertainty, valuations being still high, and technicals overstretched, a post earnings pullback would be very natural.

We have a bearish MACD crossover that just took place, and that generally leads to some weakness.

The 50 week EMA sits at $150, and I wouldn't be surprised to see Nvidia test somewhere close to those levels in coming weeks.

At that price, Nvidia would be more attractive, and I’d consider buying depending on what I see from the latest earnings.

Final Thoughts

Nvidia is the gold standard of AI, which makes their earnings so much more important. If Nvidia disappoints, then this could actually lead to a chain reaction that could be very bearish for AI stocks.

However, I’m still very excited to see what surprises Nvidia and Jensen have in store for us.

data.title: Markets Price In Another Fed Rate Cut Next Week, But Friday's Delayed Inflation Report Could Muddy The Waters
data.description:
By James Picerno

Market-based forecasts continue to expect the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates again next week at the Oct. 29 policy meeting. But getting from here to there requires going through tomorrow’s release of the delayed consumer inflation report for September.

Economists are looking for a mixed bag of numbers. The headline measure of the consumer price index (CPI) is on track to pick up to a 3.1% annual rate, the highest in nearly a year-and-a-half, according to Econoday.com’s survey. Core CPI, which strips out volatile food and energy prices and tends to be a more reliable measure of the trend, is projected to remain steady, also at 3.1%.

Assuming the estimates are correct, the CPI report will provide support for inflation hawks and doves, if only on the margins. The question is whether the update will strengthen or derail the case for another rate cut next week?

Policy is still moderately tight

TMC Research’s Fed funds model continues to indicate that monetary policy remains moderately tight. The effective Fed funds rate has been holding steady recently at roughly one percentage point above our neutral estimate. The gap leaves room for the Fed to cut while still leaving policy with a hawkish bias.

If there are concerns about upside inflation risk, it’s not showing up in the US 10-year Treasury yield, which continues to trade near its lowest level of the year.

Instead, the market and the Fed appear to remain more concerned about downside risks for the labor market. Last week Federal Reserve Chairman Powell said: “Inflation is above target and gently rising” while “the labor market is subject to pretty clear downside risks” in terms of hiring.

Labor market issues

Deciding if labor market risk remains a pressing concern, or not, has become challenging due to the government shutdown, which has delayed the September payrolls report.

Private-sector data can help fill in the gap, but alternative sources aren’t providing a clear picture of the labor market at the moment. The ADP Employment Report shows that private employers cut jobs for a second month in a row in September. Revelio Labs, a data analytics firm, paints a modestly brighter view and reports that hiring picked up last month, albeit from a low level.

Tomorrow’s CPI report could bring clarity, at least in terms of whether the Fed has a stronger or weaker case for more rate cuts. But if the consensus forecast is accurate, the inflation data will still leave room for debate.

This much is clear: the market will be forced to wait for the government to open for a clearer read on the numbers. Even if the shutdown ended today, tabulating and publishing new data will take time. The Fed, meanwhile, will be forced to make a new policy decision next Wednesday, well before the government starts updating economic figures.

An added complication for analyzing economic conditions: on top of the loss of data updates from various government agencies due to the shutdown, the payroll services firm ADP Research stopped providing its private-sector jobs data to the central bank following an Aug. 28 speech by Fed Governor Christopher Waller that referenced the numbers, according to The Wall Street Journal. The Fed’s already challenging job of assessing the economy seems to be getting tougher.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: Cipher Mining: A Solid Bitcoin Play, Though Not Without Risks
data.description:
Cipher Mining Inc. (NASDAQ:CIFR) is a Bitcoin mining and AI high-performance computing technology company. This is a dual, well-diversified business model that uses large-scale data centers for both Bitcoin mining operations and HPC hosting.

The CIFR stock price appreciated by roughly 236% over the past 12 months and significantly outperformed the benchmark. This resulted from Bitcoin appreciation and a pivot to a high-demand artificial intelligence landscape. The company has become less dependent on Bitcoin volatility, which the market has rewarded with a stock price increase.

Although I think that the decision to pivot to AI HPC has been the right one, I believe the CIFR stock price may have run ahead of its fundamentals. Despite the pivot to AI hosting, the company remains highly dependent on BTC, and with the current volatility, sentiment may change. Bitcoin seasonality could still be in play, but I think investors may shift to other legacy miners who have decided to pursue this high-demand landscape, too, but have not been rewarded with a premium yet.

This is why I think Cipher Mining is a Hold with a $20 price target over the next 12 months. I believe this company may be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors who seek data center and Bitcoin exposure via the equity market, but I also think that investors could choose other alternatives available on the market, which could weigh on the stock price in the near term.

A Pivot To AI HPC Is A Game-Changer, But I Argue The Market Priced In Too High A Premium, Too Early

Cipher Mining is one of a few legacy Bitcoin miners that have decided to shift and diversify its business to AI HPC operations. However, CIFR and Iren Limited (NASDAQ:IREN) were the ones that aggressively shifted to this landscape, and the market has rewarded them with a significant premium. However, I think that investors may have priced in too much, slightly too early, and that could weigh on the stock price.

The chart above indicates that Cipher Mining has been the second-best performing legacy Bitcoin miner. Together with IREN, they have significantly outperformed CleanSpark and MARA Holdings, mainly due to an early shift to the AI HPC landscape. It does not necessarily mean that CIFR or IREN need to come down, but there appears to be a gap in valuations, which supports my thesis that the stock market may have run ahead of its fundamentals.

The chart below reveals the comparison of the following in terms of the total market capitalization. This appears to be interesting, highlighting that CIFR has roughly $7.11 billion in market cap, which is slightly below MARA Holdings, and about 30% above CLSK.

In terms of revenue (TTM), Cipher Mining appears to be the smallest Bitcoin and AI HPC play. CIFR generated roughly $159 million over the past 12 months, which is nearly 3x below CleanSpark's approximately $632 million and more than 4x lower than MARA Holdings of nearly $799 million. This indicates that there appears to be a gap in valuations, which does not necessarily result in CIFR stock decline, but may indicate that there are better alternatives in the market, which could weigh on the stock price.

Furthermore, MARA Holdings is the second-largest publicly listed Bitcoin treasury in the world with more than 53k coins. This equals about $5.7 billion in cash at the Bitcoin price of $108,000. CLSK has more than 13k coins, and CIFR holds roughly 1.5k Bitcoins in its treasury. This supports my thesis of a significant gap in valuations.

Cipher Mining produced 251 Bitcoin in September, while MARA produced 736 BTC and CLSK 629 BTC, highlighting a massive underperformance in terms of its mining operations and smaller scale versus its peers.

This is why I think that investors may shift to other alternatives in the market, which have not appreciated that significantly in their stock price, although following the same playbook, highlighting a pivot to the AI HPC landscape. There is also a possibility that if AI HPC demand continues, Cipher Mining may continue to appreciate in the stock price.

For instance, the high-performance computing market for AI was valued at about $112 billion in 2024, and is expected to triple in size over the next 4 years and reach roughly $360 billion by 2029. This highlights a 26.3% CAGR. If this materializes, then one could argue that CIFR remains cheap at the current valuations.

On the upside, on September 25, the company announced a long-term agreement with Fluidstack, underscoring nearly $3 billion in contracted revenue over the next 10 years. Google backstopped this and acquired nearly a 5.4% stake in the legacy Bitcoin miner. I think this deserves a higher premium, but based on my comparison, I believe it may be too elevated. This is why I think that in the near-term it could underperform its peers, as I argue that the market may have run ahead of its fundamentals.

CIFR Remains A Bitcoin Miner, And That Could Spark End-Of-Year Rally, If BTC Seasonality Plays Out

Although Cipher Mining pivoted to the artificial intelligence landscape, I think the market may continue to view it as a Bitcoin miner, and the stock price could be significantly impacted by the underlying volatility of the cryptocurrency.

On the upside, this could turn out to be favorable for CIFR, highlighting that the last quarter of the year historically has been a bullish period for the crypto environment in general, and BTC in particular.

The table above indicates that Bitcoin throughout the last 3 months of the year appreciated by 78% on average, and reflected nearly 48% median returns. So far, Bitcoin is down by 5% since the beginning of October, which may highlight that if favorable seasonality plays out, the cryptocurrency environment, including equities with crypto exposure, may experience significant upside.

I also think that the current crypto environment has additional tailwinds that fuel the bullish outlook and could spark an end-of-year rally. The current administration appears to be pro-crypto, which improves the investing landscape and encourages the adoption of digital assets. For instance, on July 18, President Trump signed the Genius Act, which establishes a framework for clearer stablecoin adoption. This is significantly important and also contributes heavily to the narrative of institutional adoption of the digital assets.

Furthermore, there are more publicly listed companies that are adopting the Bitcoin HODL strategy. Currently, Strategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) is leading with a massive amount of more than 640k BTC on its balance sheet. However, I think this substantially supports the bullish outlook for Bitcoin, as these companies may provide a floor price for the underlying by not selling their Bitcoin and continuing to accumulate coins as time passes by.

I think that although the cryptocurrency landscape currently experiences fairly negative sentiment supported by a roughly 5% Bitcoin decline, it remains a volatile industry, and with a significant and favorable shift, the seasonality could still be in play. If that materializes, I think Cipher Mining could continue appreciating in the stock price. However, if the crypto industry remains weak, this could meaningfully impact the stock price, and it could correct from these levels.

Cipher Mining Appears To Be Expensive, And I Am Not Sure Whether Fundamentals Justify It

CIFR currently trades at a forward P/E of 50.21, which highlights roughly a 2x premium versus the sector median of 25.49, and more than 2x overvaluation compared to the average company in the S&P500 at about 23.10x earnings multiple.

The chart above indicates that the current valuation also highlights a multi-year high, which supports a negative outlook for the company. This does not mean that CIFR can not trade at the current levels, but it signals that upside may be capped in the near term.

The company trades at forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales of 27.19 and 26.72, respectively, which underscores a 7x premium valuation compared to peers of 3.63 and 3.57. This implies that the market has priced in the future growth, and supports my thesis that investors may have run ahead of its fundamentals.

Cipher Mining appears to be a medium-leveraged business with total obligations of $189 million. The company has about $63 million in cash that partially offsets its debt, but leverage remains elevated. To highlight, this does not reflect its recent upsized private offering, which should translate to an estimate of $1.08 billion, which is expected to be used for data center expansion. Therefore, this underscores that CIFR may continue trading alongside other interest rate-sensitive names. The Fed anticipates reducing interest rates over the next two meetings by 0.25% each, and such a dovish monetary policy could be favorable for Cipher.

The legacy Bitcoin miner barely grew its revenue over the past 12 months, indicating a 0.11% increase and massive underperformance versus the sector median of 7.12%. Although the market anticipates double-digit growth, fueled by a pivot to AI hosting in the years ahead, this also supports my thesis that the market may have priced a significant premium slightly too early, limiting upside in the short term. Nevertheless, the company grew Capex by 530% over the past 12 months, and that highlights substantial focus on a high-demand AI landscape.

The company operates at below-market margins, and that is another argument for short-term consolidation. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 21.36% indicates more than 2x underperformance versus peers at 49.98%. The EBIT margin (TTM) of negative 69% underscores the disadvantage versus the sector median of 6.45% growth. I believe margins may improve with successful scale in the AI HPC landscape, but it also comes with execution risk. If AI demand weakens or does not translate into significant top and bottom-line growth, then current valuations may appear to be too inflated.

Wall Street forecasts a $0.36 in diluted EPS for FY2025, and $0.66 for FY2026. This would put CIFR at about 27x earnings multiple for the FY2026 figure. I think that the company should be able to achieve these results, especially if BTC seasonality plays out, or at least it continues trading at above the 100k mark over the next year. The recent agreement with Fluidstack appears to be promising and supports a bullish stance for the company.

I think that AI demand may continue further, but I also believe that the current forward P/E of more than 50x for FY2025 appears to be too elevated. If we take a $0.66 EPS estimate for FY2026, and apply a roughly 30x earnings multiple, we come in at my price target of nearly $20 over the next 12 months. This would signal 10% upside, but would likely underperform peers and reflect the benchmark performance.

Conclusion: Cipher Mining Appears Compelling, But I Think There May Be Better Alternatives In The Market

I think that Cipher Mining may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors who favor a high-growth AI landscape of data centers and crypto exposure via the equity market. However, based on my analysis, I would argue there may be better alternatives among legacy Bitcoin miners, which have also pivoted to AI HPC, but have not experienced a massive surge in the stock price.

CIFR is also tied to execution risk, remains highly dependent on Bitcoin volatility, and future AI demand. At the current valuations, and after a massive triple-digit surge over the past 12 months, I think it may be extremely vulnerable if sentiment dramatically shifts. On the other hand, if AI-related demand continues, and BTC seasonality plays out, I believe CIFR could continue trading at an elevated multiple, especially if scaling in AI data centers plays out well.

This is why I think Cipher Mining is a Hold with a $20 price target over the next 12 months. This indicates 10% upside possibility, yet in line with the benchmark. 13 Wall Street analysts forecast a $19 price target over the next year, which presents a 5% upside opportunity. I feel more bullish, as I think that AI appears to be revolutionary, and data center services play a crucial role in the landscape. Time will tell, but after significant performance, I would not be surprised if the stock consolidates from here.

data.title: Viking Therapeutics: The Prime Target In The Obesity Gold Rush
data.description:
Introduction

On my quest for the best pharmaceutical companies to snatch up while the sector has been facing a downturn, I came across Viking Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:VKTX), which seems to be one of retail investors' favorites with its position as a high-risk, high-reward play. I first looked at Viking back in 2023, again as a potential takeover play, and was not expecting such a rollercoaster ride in its stock price since.

Currently, Viking Therapeutics is a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company that focuses on developing therapies for metabolic and endocrine disorders, driven by the Blockbuster potential of VK2735, a dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist. It has garnered a lot of M&A talks considering the high value of its drug pipeline.

Drug Pipeline

As the company does not currently have business segments, strictly speaking, it is hard to do my usual operation overview. However, that leaves us the chance to overview the company’s current pipeline.

VK2735 is currently the company’s most prominent program acting in the well known and covered glucagon-like peptide 1, or GLP-1, as well as the GIP receptor that has the same mechanism of action as Eli Lilly’s (LLY) Mounjaro and Zepbound. Viking is currently developing two different formulations, one injectable and another oral. The injectable VK2735 is currently in phase 3 clinical trials, with the preceding VENTURE trial demonstrating patients having up to 14.7% mean weight loss after 13 weeks of treatment. The Oral VK2735 is currently undergoing Phase 2 clinical trials, with its most recent study showing up to 12.2% mean weight loss after 13 weeks. This should directly put Viking as a direct key competitor to Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk (NVO) in the multibillion dollar race for anti-obesity drugs. VK2735 is currently why so many investors are bullish on the company, and why many believe that Viking could be a takeover target.

Viking is currently also developing VK2809, a drug designed to treat non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH). It just completed a Phase 2b trial that has met its primary endpoint and showed a significant reduction in liver fat content in patients. Viking developed another oral drug, VK0214, targeting a rare genetic neurological disorder, and is currently at earlier stages than the previous drugs, with a Phase 1b trial just being completed, showing that it was safe, well-tolerated, and reduced VLCFAs.

Current Dynamics

Viking Therapeutics just released its quarterly earnings, and though at the current stage of the company makes it difficult to assess, it posted a Q3 GAAP EPS loss of 81 cents, a miss of 11 cents due to the increase in R&D expenses, which now leaves the company with $715MM in cash and equivalents. Management has stated that this is sufficient for the time being, including the completion of the phase 3 trials for VK2735.

Most were looking toward the current trials, with Viking stating that the enrollment in the first phase 3 trials for its obesity pill VK2735 should be finished by December. The second trial should complete enrollment in Q1 2026 as well. For the Oral VK2735, the company will meet with the FDA in Q4 to discuss the next steps forward, mainly whether it can move towards a Phase 3 program. In all, I believe that the quarter was fairly good for shareholders, though yes, the increased burn rate is concerning; I believe that it is normal for such a late-stage company. The trials continue on their strong footing, and the company is on track to be commercially viable.

Outside of earnings, I want to touch on the M&A speculation that has been supporting the current stock valuation. VKTX initially jumped drastically back in early 2024 after Viking released its Phase 2 data for the injectable version of VK2735, which followed rampant market rumors that Pfizer (PFE) was in talks to acquire the company. The foundation of the rumors was fairly strong, based on Pfizer being desperate at the time and still now to deploy its vast cash resources to enter growth therapeutic areas such as weight-loss and obesity treatments. Acquiring Viking could have been a great and effective way to enter the market, and I still believe that it was the better choice over Metsera, that has fetched a total potential value of around $7.3B while “only” having its lead drug being in Phase 2. This lets us speculate on the valuation that Viking could fetch on the market, with the current valuation offering a discount.

I still believe that the M&A play is well on the table, with Viking being the last, and in my opinion, the most valuable independent late-stage company in the obesity space. If a giant like Merck (MRK) would want to enter the space, it would take 5 to 10 years and billions in R&D to create a GLP-1 drug and get it to Phase 3. Now, with each passing quarter, the company is essentially de-risking itself, becoming a better acquisition target.

The most cited companies that could take over Viking have been Merck, AstraZeneca (AZN), and Sanofi (SNY), which all have the capabilities to buy it as well as a strong presence in similar fields but lacking a GLP-1 asset. Mainly, Merck is in a race to replace Keytruda as its cash cow, as it is facing patent expiration, and Viking has the potential to become a blockbuster for the company. Pfizer could still buy VKTX, as Metsera assets are a long way from becoming commercially viable, and VKTX could provide a more direct way for commercial launch.

Valuation

Well, valuation-wise, there is not that much to say, unfortunately, as the company does not generate revenue or profits. So you won’t have my classic segments accompanied by my peer comparison graphs. However, based on the recent deal prices, premiums have been high, especially considering the Roche and Carmot deal that fetched a deal of up to $3.1B for a key drug entering only Phase 2 back then.

Now, investing in Viking Therapeutics would be highly speculative considering the lack of financial fundamentals for the company; that’s a certainty. However, I’m highly confident that the company’s current pipeline is one of the best value propositions in the healthcare sector, especially considering the current spotlight on weight-loss drugs.

Risks

As an investment would be highly speculative in Viking, I have to list more risks than usual. The main risk would be linked to clinical trial failure, with the main blow being a failure for VK2735, both injectable and oral, which would be devastating for the company’s current valuation.

Viking is also producing a drug that will fight in an intense competition environment, which is dominated by established giants like Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk, that have already highly effective drugs and have incredible manufacturing capabilities and strong market presence. Dethroning them, as well as the new entrants, could certainly be too tough a task for VKTX.

If Viking goes at it alone, it will face major hurdles in terms of manufacturing and commercial abilities, which will require another round of financing, with the biggest probability being share dilution, to the detriment of current shareholders.

Conclusion

I believe that with each passing minute, Viking is de-risking itself for potential takeovers, and though yes, investing in a potential M&A play is highly risky, it is rare to find such a pipeline gem at such a late stage in this particular industry still available on the market. For high-risk-tolerant investors, I rate Viking Therapeutics, Inc. stock a Buy.
